perturbation.original_text: Contribution by Sponsor: In consideration for the right to sponsor the Kaya Fest and to be acknowledged by Fruit of Life Productions LLC., as a Promoter of the event during the term of this Agreement, Sponsor agrees to contract with Fruit of Life Productions LLC., for the amount of $250,000.00 to be paid in full upon signing of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Contribution by Sponsor: In consideration for the right to sponsor the Kaya Fest and to be acknowledged by Fruit of Life Productions LLC., as a Promoter of the event during the term of this Agreement, Sponsor agrees to contract with Fruit of Life Productions LLC., for the amount of $250,000.00 to be paid within 90 days of signing this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 'within 90 days of signing' contradicts Florida Statute 215.422, which requires payment within a shorter timeframe for state-related contracts, creating a potential conflict.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 215.422 mandates specific payment timelines for state agencies and vendors. By allowing payment 'within 90 days,' the modified text potentially violates this statute if Fruit of Life Productions LLC is considered a state entity or the agreement falls under the purview of this law, as it may exceed the allowable payment timeframe established by the statute.

Home > Laws > 2023 Florida Statutes > Title XIV > Chapter 215 > Section 422 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2023 Florida Statutes (Including 2023C) Title XIV TAXATION AND FINANCE Chapter 215 FINANCIAL MATTERS: GENERAL PROVISIONS Entire Chapter SECTION 422 Payments, warrants, and invoices; processing time limits; dispute resolution; agency or judicial branch compliance. F.S. 215.422 215.422 Payments, warrants, and invoices; processing time limits; dispute resolution; agency or judicial branch compliance. — (1) An invoice submitted to an agency of the state or the judicial branch, required by law to be filed with the Chief Financial Officer, shall be recorded in the financial systems of the state, approved for payment by the agency or the judicial branch, and filed with the Chief Financial Officer not later than 20 days after receipt of the invoice and receipt, inspection, and approval of the goods or services, except that in the case of a bona fide dispute the invoice recorded in the financial systems of the state shall contain a statement of the dispute and authorize payment only in the amount not disputed. The Chief Financial Officer may establish dollar thresholds and other criteria for all invoices and may delegate to a state agency or the judicial branch responsibility for maintaining the official invoices and documents for invoices which do not exceed the thresholds or which meet the established criteria. Such records shall be maintained in accordance with the requirements established by the Secretary of State. The transmission of an approved invoice recorded in the financial systems of the state to the Chief Financial Officer shall constitute filing of a request for payment of invoices for which the Chief Financial Officer has delegated to an agency custody of official records. Approval and inspection of goods or services shall take no longer than 5 working days unless the bid specifications, purchase order, or contract specifies otherwise. If an invoice filed within the 20-day period is returned by the Department of Financial Services because of an error, it shall nevertheless be deemed timely filed. The 20-day filing requirement may be waived in whole or in part by the Department of Financial Services on a showing of exceptional circumstances in accordance with rules and regulations of the department. For the purposes of determining t
perturbation.justification: The original text requires payment 'upon signing,' while the changed text allows payment 'within 90 days.' The Law Explanation states that Florida Statute 215.422 mandates specific payment timelines. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that invoices should be processed and filed with the Chief Financial Officer 'not later than 20 days after receipt of the invoice and receipt, inspection, and approval of the goods or services.' Extending the payment window to 90 days directly contradicts the 20-day requirement, making the modified contract non-compliant and creating a legal contradiction, as defined by the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this agreement will begin on April 1, 2018 and continue until April 30, 2018 at 11:59pm.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this agreement will begin on April 1, 2018 and continue until April 2, 2018 at 11:59pm.
perturbation.explanation: The agreement is shortened to only two days, which may violate Florida Statute 725.06 which relates to the right of cancellation of certain contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: By reducing the contract duration to just two days, the modified text may not afford consumers sufficient time to exercise their right to cancel contracts as stipulated under certain consumer protection laws. The sponsor is agreeing to give a lot of money for effectively no time, since it will take time to put up booths, banners, etc.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the contract duration to two days. This directly contradicts the Law Explanation, which states that reducing the contract duration may not afford consumers sufficient time to exercise their right to cancel contracts as stipulated under certain consumer protection laws. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a deadline or requirement violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal. The original contract allowed for a longer period (April 1 to April 30), offering more opportunity for cancellation if such a right exists. The changed contract significantly shortens this timeframe, creating a potential conflict with consumer protection laws related to cancellation rights.

perturbation.original_text: In case of a dispute, the parties agree to pursue Arbitration as the preferred method to seek a remedy and the parties waive the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.changed_text: In case of a dispute, the parties agree to pursue Arbitration exclusively and waive the right to seek any other form of legal remedy, including but not limited to, court proceedings or jury trial.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing exclusive arbitration and waiving all other legal remedies, even for claims involving statutory rights or consumer protection, may violate Florida Statute 542.335, especially if the arbitration clause is deemed unconscionable or restricts remedies available under the statute.
perturbation.law_explanation: By mandating exclusive arbitration and waiving all other legal remedies, the modified text potentially violates Florida Statute 542.335 if it unduly restricts the remedies available to parties under the statute, especially in cases involving non-compete agreements or other disputes where the statute provides specific rights and remedies. A complete waiver of court proceedings could be deemed unenforceable if it deprives a party of statutory rights or protections.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Florida Statutes § 542.335 (2022) - Valid Restraints of Trade or Commerce. :: 2022 Florida Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Florida Stat. 2023 Florida Stat. 2022 Florida Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Florida Statutes Title XXXIII - Regulation of Trade, Commerce, Investments, and Solicitations Chapter 542 - Combinations Restricting Trade or Commerce 542.335 - Valid Restraints of Trade or Commerce. Universal Citation: FL Stat § 542.335 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 542.335 Valid restraints of trade or commerce.— (1) Notwithstanding s. 542.18 and subsection (2), enforcement of contracts that restrict or prohibit competition during or after the term of restrictive covenants, so long as such contracts are reasonable in time, area, and line of business, is not prohibited. In any action concerning enforcement of a restrictive covenant: (a) A court shall not enforce a restrictive covenant unless it is set forth in a writing signed by the person against whom enforcement is sought. (b) The person seeking enforcement of a restrictive covenant shall plead and prove the existence of one or more legitimate business interests justifying the restrictive covenant. The term “legitimate business interest” includes, but is not limited to: 1. Trade secrets, as defined in s. 688.002(4). 2. Valuable confidential business or professional information that otherwise does not qualify as trade secrets. 3. Substantial relationships with specific prospective or existing customers, patients, or clients. 4. Customer, patient, or client goodwill associated with: a. An ongoing business or professional practice, by way of trade name, trademark, service mark, or “trade dress”; b. A specific geographic location; or c. A specific marketing or trade area. 5. Extraordinary o
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates exclusive arbitration, waiving all other legal remedies, which potentially violates Florida Statute 542.335 by unduly restricting remedies available under the statute. The law explanation states that the complete waiver of court proceedings could be deemed unenforceable if it deprives a party of statutory rights or protections. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing Florida Statutes § 542.335 (2022), concerns 'Valid Restraints of Trade or Commerce.' While this snippet focuses on restrictive covenants and legitimate business interests, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that forcing exclusive arbitration and waiving all other remedies might violate this statute, especially if the arbitration clause restricts remedies available under the statute or is unconscionable, thus creating a contradiction.
